questions.question: What is the key difference between Level 2 and Level 3 conversational depth?

options.text: Level 2 is facts and logistics; Level 3 is preferences
Level 2 is preferences and opinions; Level 3 is vulnerable sharing (moderate personal depth)
There is no difference — they're the same
Level 3 is always inappropriate in adult friendships

questions.explanation: Level 2 is preferences, opinions, and interests ('I love running in the cold — most people think I'm crazy'). Level 3 is vulnerable sharing that signals trust and invites emotional reciprocity ('I've been feeling pretty isolated since I moved here. Making friends as an adult is harder than I expected'). The progression from Level 2 to Level 3 requires: (1) consistent reciprocity at Level 2, (2) mutual openness signals, and (3) gradual escalation, not sudden oversharing.

questions.question: What is active-constructive responding?

options.text: Responding to someone's good news with subdued acknowledgment ('That's good')
Pointing out potential downsides when someone shares good news
Enthusiastic engagement with follow-up questions when someone shares good news ('That's amazing! Tell me more!')
Ignoring the good news and changing the subject

questions.explanation: Active-constructive responding is enthusiastic, engaged, and asks deepening questions ('That's amazing! How did it happen? How are you feeling?'). Research shows this strengthens relationships MORE than how you respond to bad news. The other styles: passive-constructive (supportive but subdued), active-destructive (points out downsides), and passive-destructive (ignores or changes subject) all weaken relationships. Amplify their joy, show genuine interest, and celebrate with them.

questions.question: What is the first step in the conflict repair process?

options.text: Immediately apologize and take full blame even if you don't know what happened
Pretend nothing happened and hope it goes away
Acknowledge the rupture explicitly ('I noticed things felt off between us after our conversation last week')
Demand that the other person apologize first

questions.explanation: Step 1 is acknowledging the rupture. Don't pretend nothing happened or avoid the discomfort. Name it: 'I noticed things felt off between us.' This signals that the relationship matters to you enough to address difficulty. Then: (2) take responsibility for your part, (3) ask about their experience, (4) validate their feelings, (5) clarify intention vs. impact, (6) commit to behavioral change, and (7) rebuild trust through demonstrated follow-through.

questions.question: Why is matching disclosure depth important in reciprocal self-disclosure?

options.text: It's not important — you should always share as deeply as possible
Oversharing relative to the other person's comfort level can feel overwhelming or boundary-violating; gradual escalation feels safer
You should never share anything personal
Matching depth is manipulative and should be avoided

questions.explanation: Matching depth respects the other person's comfort and readiness. If they're at Level 2 (preferences) and you jump to Level 4 (very deep personal sharing), it can feel like too much too soon — overwhelming or boundary-violating. If you share at Level 3 and they respond with Level 1 (facts only), they're signaling they're not ready for depth. Respect that and dial back. Gradual escalation with reciprocity builds trust safely. This isn't manipulation — it's attuned connection.

questions.question: What is the evidence-based reality about maintaining long-distance friendships?

options.text: Good friendships maintain themselves without any effort
Real friends can go years without talking and pick up exactly where they left off
All friendships require intentional maintenance through consistent communication; 'effortless' friendships are rare
Long-distance friendships always fail

questions.explanation: All friendships require intentional maintenance, especially across distance. Consistent communication (scheduled calls, small updates, showing up for milestones) sustains connection. 'Picking up where we left off' after months of silence is possible in rare friendships with strong history, but the current connection is often weak. Effort doesn't mean the friendship is struggling — it means you value it enough to maintain it. Schedule recurring calls, share small touches, plan visits in advance, and accept natural ebb and flow.

title: Conversational Depth and Maintenance Skills - Quiz
